data.title: Vontier: The Hidden Industrial Software Moat
data.description:
Vontier corporation (VNT) offers industrial software embedded within physical machinery and equipment and controls the physical layer of operational processes, whether it's a fuel pump, a car wash, or even a power charger.

While some bears argue that AI will replace software companies, the unified payment system VNT offers through FlexPay 6 and iNFX solves a painful problem for shop owners and gas stations, as they need systems that integrate loyalty programs, media displays, and ordering processes. Vontier offers them the solution through its microservices architecture, which allows them to unify the user interface and connect internal POS terminals to external payment terminals.

Additionally, the payment systems go through complex stages and require multiple layers of security certifications, which could cost clients millions of dollars. This interconnectedness of an ecosystem unifies payments whether in-store, cashier, and car wash makes abandoning Vontier’s software unlikely and significantly increases the switching costs for customers.

Financials & Outlook

During Q4, the company achieved total revenues of $808.5 million and recorded a core growth of %5.1 YoY.The Adj EPS reached $0.86 with 8% increase compared to the same quarter last year. The full year organic sales grew by 4% plus the EPS rose by 11% to $3.20. What matters is reading these figures in the context of each segment.

Mobility Technologies: The Growth Engine

Mobility Technologies represents the SaaS ecosystem of the company, including payment systems and fleet management. It achieved a growth of 8.5%, ending the year with $1.12 billion and an 11% increase YoY. The top performance came from Invenco, which closed the year with $650 million in revenues, recording a growth of 22%, reflecting strong demand for VNT’s payment technologies and the wide adoption of its NFX architecture.

While the operating margin declined by 220 basis points to reach 18.5% due to a one-time inventory adjustment at Invenco, this is generally a healthy sign, meaning that customers are rapidly adopting newer solutions such as FlexPay 6, as the old equipment became obsolete.

Additionally, the car wash solutions witnessed a significant improvement with high single-digit growth in revenues driven by adopting Patheon software, as customers who upgraded to Patheon saw a mid-teens revenue growth on average and a decrease in cancellations in parallel.

Repair Solutions

This segment faced some challenges during 2025 as total revenues declined by 7% to reach $590 million. This decline is primarily due to macroeconomic pressures on consumers, as they reduced their spending on expensive equipment such as toolboxes.

Historically, Q4 is the slowest for this segment. However, it showed some positive signs, as it recorded sequential growth compared to Q3. Importantly, the actual sales from distribution trucks turned positive for the first time in the year due to management's focus on diagnostic tools that achieved low double-digit growth.

While the technicians are reducing their spending on expensive luxuries, they are willing to invest in diagnostic tools that save them time, thus allowing them to repair more cars per day and increase their income. Since Matco Tools focuses on these productivity tools, it provides VNT with flexibility against economic headwinds.

Fueling Equipment & Services

This includes the popular Gilbarco Veeder-Root brand, which is the mainstay of VNT's cash flows. It achieved a 6% growth with total revenues of $1.43 billion. The growth was broadband as it included investments in new sites and upgrading old equipment above and below ground.

Liquidity & Guidance

In addition, the company generated operating cash of $511 million. In addition to adjusted free cash flows of $462.3 million, which is 26% growth YoY, reflecting an exceptional cash conversion rate representing 15% of total sales. This significant liquidity provides the company with the necessary flexibility to pursue its strategies. The company also benefited from the OBBB, which resulted in a 30 million tax reduction in 2025, and it will add an additional 30 million in 2026, which should directly translate into free cash flow.

Furthermore, the company repurchased 8 million shares for $300 million, which is a sign from management that the stock is undervalued. They also have authorization to buy up to $500 million worth of shares.

Revenues are expected to be between $3.10 and $3.15 billion, representing a growth of 3%. Operating margins are also projected to expand by 80 basis points, with adjusted EPS ranging between $3.35 and $3.50. However, the first half of the year is going to be slower than the previous year due to non-recurring projects delivered in the first half of 2025. So the strong growth should be in the second half, when the company begins to deliver the new projects and the seasonal sales of MatcoTools grow.

Value Per Share

VNT ended the year with total debt of $2.1 billion and cash of $492.2 million, resulting in a net debt of $1.6 billion and a net leverage ratio of 2.3x compared to 2.6x last year. Consequently, EBITDA is $695 million. While the company trade at an EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 9x, its peers trade at 13x-14x. Assuming a conservative multiple of 10x as the market has a historical perception of Vontier as a legacy hardware provider and current concerns over short-term organic growth, the enterprise value comes to $6.95 billion. Therefore, the equity value is approximately $5.35 billion or $37.4 per share.

While the software currently represents 10%-12% of total revenue, if the company demonstrates the success of its new connected mobility strategy, which is shifting from selling siloed physical products to offering interconnected technology solutions, such as Unified Payment and Patheon software, the market will start to reprice it as an industrial technology company, justifying giving it a higher multiple.

Risks To Consider

The most notable challenge for VNT is the rapid adoption of electric vehicles, which will impact or even eliminate gas stations. However, moving to EVs won't happen overnight. It is a long process that will take decades, as the average life span of a car in the United States is 12.8 years. Vontier is also investing in this transition through Driivz which manages global charging networks for EVs and approximately handles one terawatt of power. Also, some gas stations are gradually adopting the EV chargers where Vontier is there to provide them with the necessary software.

The repair sector is very sensitive to recessions. Therefore, any economic slowdown will continue the pressure on MatcoTools margins and could lead technicians to default on loans. However, the company's focus on diagnostic tools, which generate quick returns, should mitigate any decline in heavy equipment sales.

Final Thoughts

Vontier achieved excellent results during the last quarter, particularly in the Mobility Technologies segment, thanks to its unified payment system. It also has essential products that add value for technicians even in economic downturns, represented in diagnostic tools. The company also sent a buy signal to investors through recent buybacks of $300 million. However, it's important for the company to continue expanding its customer base adoption of Patheon and unified payment system, which is the growth key for the future. Additionally, we should monitor the sales of diagnostic tools, which should offset the headwinds facing MatcoTools.

data.title: National Healthcare Properties Files For IPO As Business Model Shifts
data.description:
National Healthcare Properties Is A Company In Transition

National Healthcare Properties (NHP) has filed to raise capital from an IPO to pay down debt, according to an S-11 registration statement and related 10-K financial statement.

The REIT acquires and operates medical offices and senior housing facilities in the United States.

NHP is shifting its focus to senior housing but appears to still be in mid-transition, presenting risks for investors.

What Does National Healthcare Properties Do?

The company acquires and manages a diversified portfolio of senior housing operating properties and outpatient medical facilities in the U.S.

As of the end of 2025, NHP owned 167 properties and a parcel of land in 29 states, comprised of 37 senior housing [SHOP] facilities and 130 outpatient medical locations [OMF], as shown on the footprint map below:

The firm’s total number of properties has fallen in recent years, from 202 in 2023 to 192 in 2024 and 167 in 2025.

Management chooses to retain properties in markets that it believes will have ‘strong senior population growth and robust healthcare infrastructure.’

In its senior housing segment, the firm counts only three operators, as shown here:

Its outpatient medical segment features five major tenants plus smaller tenants in the remaining portfolio, as detailed below:

NHP is led by President and Chief Executive Officer Michael Anderson, who has been with the company since September 2023 and joined AR Global Investments in 2013.

AR Global is the successor to AR Capital [ARC], which founded (with Nicholas Schorsch) American Realty Capital in 2006. American Realty was among other REITs founded by ARC that had various accounting, governance, and distribution issues.

Investors have invested fair market value of approximately $2.5 billion as of the end of 2025.

NHP is very similar to recently public Janus Living (JAN), in that the two firms operate their senior housing under RIDEA structures, which means that ‘our interest in these communities is not limited to contractual rental payments, and we are able to benefit from any operating improvements at all our senior housing communities (while at the same time bearing the risk of any potential operating declines).' (Source - SEC)

What are NHP’s Markets?

NHP operates in two distinct markets, senior housing and outpatient medical facilities.

According to a Grand View Research market research report published in 2026, the U.S. senior living market (all housing types) was an estimated $944 billion in 2025 and was forecasted to exceed $1.3 trillion by 2033.

If accomplished, this growth would equate to a CAGR of nearly 4.5% from 2026 to 2033.

With the retirement of the Baby Boom generation in the U.S., around 10,000 people are retiring each day, so the senior living market will be propelled forward by this inescapable demographic reality.

The chart below shows the expected growth trajectory of the U.S. Senior Living Market, from 2023 through 2033, by facility type:

Competitors in the U.S. senior housing market include:

  • Janus Living (JAN)

  • Ventas (VTR)

  • Welltower (WELL)

  • Enlivant

  • Brookdale Senior Living (BKD)

  • Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC)

  • Santere Health Investors

  • Harrison Street Real Estate

  • American Healthcare REIT (AHR)

  • ReNew REIT

As for NHP, its SHOP assets provide higher long-term growth potential, but they have higher operating volatility, resulting in the possibility of greater earnings variability.

The firm is also active in the outpatient medical facility market, which is enjoying increasing investment interest due to a variety of trends, according to a 2026 Healthcare Dive report.

The report cites a combination of demographic demand, economics and federal policy changes encouraging the development of outpatient or ambulatory medical facilities.

Not only is the senior population growing, as previously mentioned, but federal reimbursement policies are changing to allow more procedures to be performed outside of hospitals, reducing costs in the process.

NHP’s strengths include favorable industry tailwinds, limited new supply of senior housing and outpatient facilities, and almost exclusively private pay for its SHOP portfolio.

The company's weaknesses include a sub-scale size compared to public peers, heavy SHOP operator concentration, and capital recycling timing delays

NHP’s opportunities are to acquire SHOP assets in a fragmented market, recycling OMF assets into higher growth SHOP locations, improving SHOP margins from increasing occupancy, improved pricing and labor efficiencies

Threats to the firm’s business include staffing shortage and labor cost inflation, higher interest rates, regulatory scrutiny and changes and geographic concentration in a small number of states.

NHP’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s financial results for 2024 and 2025 are shown in the table below.

Total revenue fell slightly, as did net operating income, as the company divested some OMF assets and is in the process of recycling that capital.

However, normalized funds from operations rose substantially year-over-year:

NHP’s IPO Plan

The company has filed an S-11 registration statement with a placeholder SEC review calculation figure of $100 million, although the final amount of the IPO may be higher.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus, as determined by the SEC.

Management said the net proceeds of the IPO will be used as follows:

We will contribute the net proceeds from this offering to the Operating Partnership in exchange for Common OP Units. We expect the Operating Partnership to use the net proceeds received from us to repay [an as-yet undetermined amount] of outstanding indebtedness under the Revolving Facility and use such remaining proceeds to fund external growth with potential future property acquisitions and for other general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC)

The company appears to be going public now to improve its balance sheet, but that may limit accretion potential in the near term for IPO investors.

The online roadshow of the company's IPO presentation is not currently available, although the roadshow only contains information in the most recent S-11 filing.

Management said the company is not the target of any legal proceedings that would be material in amount, or the threat of same.

The listed underwriters of the IPO include Wells Fargo Securities, Morgan Stanley, BMO Capital Markets, Goldman Sachs, RBC Capital Markets, Baird, Capital One Securities, Citizens Capital Markets, Fifth Third Securities, Huntington Capital Market, KeyBanc Capital Markets, Credit Agricole CIB and Synovus.

NHP Is Shifting To A Senior Housing Focus

By the end of 2025, NHP internalized management, divested some OMF assets, reduced debt and appears to be focusing future capital deployment on the higher-growth senior housing [SHOP] segment.

However, leverage remains elevated, while free cash flow is weak, and the company is still in a transition phase.

As of March 31, 2026, NHP had approximately $90.5 million in SHOP segment acquisitions ‘under letters of intent or subject to executed purchase and sale agreements.’

Its public peers have already focused on the SHOP segment, so NHP will need to increase its exposure through acquisitions as it recycles OMF capital into SHOP opportunities.

The IPO will serve to improve funding flexibility due to its immediate deleveraging result.

With competitor Janus Living's initial annual distribution yield set to 3%, NHP will likely need to match that figure to stay competitive.

The market opportunity is large, and management has correctly identified the senior housing market as having greater potential.

But, interest rates appear to be moving in the wrong direction for NHP, assuming it levers up again, such as to make a sizable acquisition.

So, while the asset strategy is improving, the balance sheet is following more slowly since the cost of capital may rise further.

For prospective investors, this IPO appears to be closer to a transition/speculative play rather than a fully de-risked compounding stock.

My overall summary of the NHP IPO so far is:

  • Positive - Industry tailwinds from strong demographic demand
  • Uncertain - Logical SHOP strategy, but execution is still in process; improving FFO but weak NOI and heavy leverage; positive deleveraging outlook, but near-term upside is limited
  • Negative - Balance sheet is heavily levered

Since the recent IPO of Janus Living was moderately well-received by investors, I suspect a similar outcome will be in play for NHP, assuming a reasonably priced IPO.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined

data.title: Tesla: Time To Dump Shares
data.description:
In the last three months since we published our recent bearish thesis on Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), the company’s stock has lost almost 20% of its value and underperformed against the market. Since that time, the situation for Tesla has only gotten worse. Shortly after we published that article, Tesla announced an annual decrease in revenue for the second year in a row. Then, at the beginning of this month, it was announced that the company’s Q1 deliveries missed expectations. As a result, we saw countless downward revisions by the analysts since there’s a risk that Tesla could once again disappoint investors and underperform in 2026.

Because of that, we continue to be pessimistic about Tesla’s future and believe that a rating of Sell makes the most sense right now.

It’s Not Getting Better

The Q1 delivery numbers that came out earlier this month were pretty disappointing. During the quarter, Tesla delivered only 358,023 vehicles, below the expectations of around 365,000 vehicles, while it also produced 408,386 cars. This indicates that while there’s no issue with producing vehicles, the demand for them is not increasing significantly. In our previous article on Tesla, we already explained that Elon Musk’s involvement in politics last year, along with the rising competition, has negatively affected Tesla’s performance, and things are unlikely to improve anytime soon.

The latest reports indicate that in Europe, Volkswagen (VWAGY) has now become the best-selling EV producer last year. At the same time, for thirteen months in a row, Tesla has experienced a decrease in registrations in Europe. Only in February did it see its first increase in registrations in a while, but there’s no guarantee that it will be able to retain the momentum in the following months due to the rise of competitors such as Volkswagen and BYD (BYDDF).

Since the automotive revenues continue to generate the absolute majority of the overall sales, there’s a risk that Tesla will once again disappoint investors this year, especially since the Q1 deliveries were below expectations. At the same time, the energy business is unlikely to offset the disappointing performance of the automotive business. This is because in Q1, deployments came in at 8.8 GWh. This is far below the 14.2 GWh record set in Q4. It’s also nowhere near the 14.4 GWh that analysts had expected. All of this makes us believe that it’s going to be extremely hard for Tesla to create additional shareholder value in the near future.

Questionable Solutions

Elon Musk’s answer to the declining car business has been to focus on autonomy and robotics. However, it’s hard to see how any advanced products are going to be quickly monetized at scale and be able to offset the underperformance of the core automotive business that’s currently focused on the selling of EVs.

In February, after eight months since its launch, Tesla’s robotaxi fleet included only around 42 vehicles. The Cybercab is expected to enter production only this month, though Musk himself has said that early volumes will be slow. Considering this, it’s hard to see how this business will be able to help turn things around for the better anytime soon. After all, CEO Elon Musk told investors back in 2019 that a million robotaxis would be on the road in 2020. Seven years after that statement was made, we’re seeing only a limited number of Tesla’s self-driving vehicles on the roads.

Then there’s Optimus. Tesla targets over $20 billion in CapEx this year, with a decent portion going toward the robot. The Fremont factory lines that used to build the Model S and Model X are being converted to produce humanoid robots instead. Last year, Musk said that 80% of Tesla’s future value will come from Optimus. We find it hard to see how the robot business turns a profit anytime soon, considering that it’s not even in the proper production stage yet. Musk may be looking a few years or even decades ahead, but the market has to deal with the business as it exists today.

In addition, Musk recently announced Terafab, which is a $25 billion chip production plant in Austin that would be a joint venture between Tesla, SpaceX (SPACE), and xAI (X.AI). It’s hard to see the project succeeding anytime soon and producing 2nm chips without previous experience in making advanced semiconductors. It took decades for TSMC (TSM) to be able to reach a point where it could start ramping up the 2nm output. That’s why it’s not a surprise that Nvidia’s (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang stated that it’s almost impossible to replicate what TSMC does when answering the question about Musk’s chip ambitions.

The financial side of Terafab is also an issue. Barclays has already noted that Terafab will push CapEx well above the $20 billion target, which Tesla guided for 2026. In addition, Morgan Stanley doesn’t expect any chips to come out of the facility before mid-2028, even under the most aggressive scenario. This is why we’re also skeptical about the ability of Tesla’s more advanced products to somehow improve the company’s current state of affairs anytime soon.

Also, all of this is happening at a time when the macro environment is experiencing a shock from the Iran war. Oil has already climbed to over $100 a barrel, while the upcoming data for March could push the inflation rate above 3%.

For Tesla specifically, higher inflation keeps the Fed from cutting rates, which means consumers are stuck with expensive financing on new vehicles. Musk made this exact point himself during 2022 and 2023. Back then, he kept saying that high interest rates were hurting Tesla because it makes it more expensive for buyers to handle the monthly payments. As such, we don’t see any upside for Tesla in the current environment.

The Real Value of Tesla

To find out Tesla’s intrinsic value in the current environment, we created a valuation model that’s available below. In the model, we used a perpetual growth rate of 3%, which is close to the historical inflation and GDP rate. The cash and debt numbers were taken from Tesla’s recent earnings report.

Tesla’s discount rate in our model is 10.85%. We figured it out by calculating the company’s cost of debt and cost of equity. In the cost of debt calculation, we used mostly Tesla’s TTM numbers. In the cost of equity valuation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.30%, a beta of 1.88, and a market return rate of 7.84%.

In January, when we were creating our previous model, our sales assumptions were close to the overall consensus at that time. Back then, the analysts estimated that Tesla’s sales growth rate in 2026 and 2027 would be 14.06% and 17.93%, respectively. After the release of the Q4 report and the announcement of Q1 deliveries, the consensus now is that the growth rate in 2026 and 2027 will only be 8.62% and 16.90%, respectively. Only closer to the terminal year the growth rate is expected to meaningfully improve. Considering this, our sales assumptions right now are similar to the latest revised consensus.

We also expect only a gradual improvement of the EBIT margin in the following years. However, the margin itself could be much lower due to the potential increase in costs, due to the increased spending on advanced products that we discussed earlier. The tax rate in the model is 21%, which is what the current corporate tax rate in the United States is. The CapEx assumptions have been increased to over $20 billion in 2026, which is what the management guided in the recent earnings call. As a result of this, the D&A assumptions have also been increased. The assumptions for the change in net working capital are similar to Tesla’s historical performance.

Our model shows that under those assumptions, Tesla’s enterprise value is $258.79 billion, while its equity value is $288.13 billion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $81.41 per share. For a company that trades at around 170 times its forward earnings, we believe that it is a fairly reasonable intrinsic value.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

Despite all of those risks, we think it’s fair to point out what could go right. As we’ve already mentioned, the Cybercab production is getting underway this month, while the robotaxi service is expected to be expanded to other cities in the foreseeable future. If we see the unsupervised FSD approved in multiple states, the revenue opportunity from ride-hailing could be meaningful over the long run.

Tesla’s performance in China is also improving. In the last two months, EV sales from the Shanghai factory were up Y/Y. Although that’s mostly because February 2025 was abysmal during the Model Y changeover. Still, the refreshed Model Y seems to be gaining traction.

Tesla also has a decent balance sheet right now. The company finished 2025 with $44.06 billion in cash and investments. That is enough to absorb a few bad quarters without financial distress. On the macro side, the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down Trump’s tariffs removed one overhang, even though a 10% replacement tariff partially offset the benefit. If oil prices come down and the Middle East situation stabilizes, some of the current macro headwinds could ease. This could push Tesla’s stock higher and slightly undermine our bearish thesis.

Final Thoughts

Although there’s a possibility that Tesla’s shares will be able to bounce in the short term, we still believe that the long-term upside is simply not there. For the last two years, the company has experienced a decline in revenues. Its Q1 deliveries were below expectations, while the performance of the energy business was also below the consensus. The solutions that are being proposed, like the development of a chip factory or the bet on full autonomy, are unlikely to be materialized anytime soon. As such, we believe that giving Tesla’s stock a rating of Sell makes the most sense at the current price.

data.title: The TJX Companies: Too High Price For An Off-Price Retailer
data.description:
The TJX Companies (TJX) has been thriving in the recent quarters and years, mainly driven by the weakness of the consumer. This may sound counterintuitive at first, but when consumer confidence is low, people normally choose cheaper alternatives when it comes to discretionary shopping. As an off-price retailer, TJX is thriving in this environment. The recent developments in the macro landscape - and here I specifically mean the outbreak of the war in Iran - may, however, pose headwinds for TJX's business. In my view, these developments and their potential implications are not currently reflected in TJX's valuation. While I fundamentally like the company, the current overvaluation, combined with the insider activity - which was largely selling - prevents me from issuing a buy rating for TJX. I currently rate TJX as a hold.

Fundamentals and the Macro Landscape

TJX did not just deliver strong results in the most recent quarter, but they have been doing so in the past several years. The table below shows how TJX's net sales and profits developed for each segment over the past three years. Over this time period, the segment revenues grew each year, as well as the segment profits, causing company-wide profitability to significantly improve.

When analyzing firms with numerous reportable segments, I generally like when the growth is broad-based and not driven by a single segment or product category. This is true for TJX. Also important to highlight that comparable sales growth contributed significantly to the net sales growth. This is important because it means that the company's sales are not only growing because they are expanding, but also because people are spending more at their existing locations.

Looking forward to fiscal 2027, TJX expects the comparable sales growth to soften, estimating the growth to be between 2% and 3%. With regard to profitability, they expect the pre-tax profit margin to be in the range of 11.7% to 11.8%, slightly lower than it was in the previous fiscal year.

In my view, these expectations are well aligned with the current macroeconomic landscape. Consumer confidence in the United States remains soft, meaning that the demand for cheaper alternatives is likely to continue, hence the expected sales growth.

The war in Iran has, however, far-reaching consequences. Since the breakout of the conflict, energy prices have skyrocketed. Brent crude is trading well above $100 per barrel. High oil prices normally lead to higher transportation and manufacturing expenses. These higher expenses can easily eat into the firm's profit if they are not able to pass on these elevated costs to their customers. We already established that TJX is thriving in the current environment because it offers a cheaper alternative to its customers. This means that their customers are price cautious and price sensitive. In my view, this means that the demand for TJX's products is also elastic, meaning that if prices were to increase, demand would likely fall. For this reason, I believe that TJX's expectation of lower profitability is also realistic and likely.

Despite the forecasted slowing of growth and forecasted deterioration of profitability, I believe that TJX remains solid fundamentally, and achieving any growth at all in the consumer discretionary sector in the current market is attractive. But how much should we be paying for this growth?

Valuation and Insider Activity

In my previous writing about TJX, the main reason I assigned the firm a hold rating was its valuation. My view has not changed since then. Currently, TJX is trading at a significant premium compared to both the consumer discretionary sector median and the firm's own historic averages. In my view, this premium is not justified.

Let us narrow down the comparison to a smaller number of firms to get a clearer picture of the valuation. First of all, TJX seems more expensive than its direct peers in the apparel retail industry, according to most metrics. One may think that TJX is not much more expensive than its peers, so the valuation may be reasonable. Well, in relative terms, maybe. But let us look at a high-growth, high-profitability company from the communication services sector - Alphabet (GOOG). GOOG is trading at significantly lower price multiples than TJX, according to most metrics. In my view, paying a P/E of 30x+ for a retailer is a bit of a stretch when you can get one of the world's best tech companies for less.

For these reasons, I do not believe that the current share price of TJX is justified and that there could be significant upside potential from the current price levels.

Last, but not least, let us look at the insider activity in the recent past. We can see that most of the activity was selling, and the total value of selling was relatively high. One may say that there were two buying activities as well. While that may be true, the number of shares bought is very low, and the share prices at which the shares were purchased are also very low. For these reasons, I do not think that the insider activity indicates that there could be much upside coming in the near future.

All in all, I do not find TJX's shares attractive from a valuation point of view.

Conclusions

The TJX Companies did not just deliver strong quarterly results in the most recent quarter, but they kept doing so in the past 3 years. Net sales, comparable sales, and profitability metrics were gradually improving. TJX, as an off-price retailer, is also well-positioned to thrive in a low consumer confidence environment.

The firm, however, is also not completely shielded from the macroeconomic headwinds. I expect elevated energy prices to bite into TJX's margins in the coming quarters. This is also reflected in their fiscal 2027 forecast.

I do not find TJX attractive from a valuation point of view. I would like to see the share price decline 25% to 30% before I would consider a rating upgrade.

For these reasons, I maintain my previously established hold rating on the firm.

data.title: VIG: March 2026 Reconstitution Reinforces Low Turnover, High Dividend Growth Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Index tracked by the $99B Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG) reconstituted last month, but unlike in 2023 and 2025, when Apple (AAPL) and Eli Lilly (LLY) crossed the ten-year dividend growth threshold to qualify, there were no noteworthy additions or deletions this year. Total portfolio turnover was just 5%, and besides some trimming of top names like Broadcom (AVGO), I have very little to report. Although not exciting from a writer's perspective, this consistency is actually excellent news for dividend growth investors, since relatively low portfolio turnover is a common denominator for nearly all U.S. Equity ETFs on 10+ year dividend growth streaks.

In addition, VIG's fundamentals look solid. Along with a 1.71% estimated dividend yield, its five-year portfolio beta is low at 0.86, and its next-year 13.67% EPS growth rate should provide a strong foundation for future dividend increases. To be sure, I haven't always been VIG's biggest fan, but I do appreciate that it consistently achieves its dividend growth objectives and often does so with less risk. Therefore, I've assigned VIG a solid "hold" rating today, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

VIG Strategy Discussion

VIG has $99B in assets, a 0.04% expense ratio, and tracks the S&P Dividend Growers Index after previously tracking the Nasdaq Dividend Achievers Index until September 2021. A summary of the changes is below, and although it's technically a different Index, I've previously determined that this Index change was not very impactful. Certain screens, like the removal of the top 25% of eligible stocks by yield, were formalized, but the requirement of 10+ years of consecutive dividend increases remains the core criterion.

VIG's turnover rates are consistently low, too. For the year ending January 31, turnover ranged from 8-12% for the last four years, and that's actually a really good sign for future dividend growth. With minimal changes at the quarterly rebalancings and annual reconstitutions, the dividend increases generated by its holdings will almost always flow straight through to VIG shareholders. In contrast, other ETFs might remove a high DGI stock at Index reconstitutions for other fundamental reasons (e.g., a too low dividend yield). That's how the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) operates, but its Index offsets this by creating more "synthetic" dividend growth by routinely selling low-yielding stocks and replacing them with higher-yielding ones.

As a DGI investor, you might not care whether your ETF generates "organic" or "synthetic" dividend growth, but I believe you should. To illustrate, I could run an ETF that rotates into the ten highest-yielding stocks every week, thereby ensuring a high dividend yield and a high dividend growth rate. However, that would say nothing about the quality of the underlying holdings. I could be buying junk stocks whose yields are high for very good reasons, and just keep buying junkier stocks each week whose yields are even higher. If you ever did decide to look beyond the basic dividend characteristics of the fund (yield and dividend growth), there's a good chance you'll be disappointed.

Dividend Growth ETFs: Low Portfolio Turnover Is The Common Denominator

According to my Dividend ETF Screener, there are only nine U.S. Size & Style ETFs on 10+ year dividend growth streaks:

  1. SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY): 16 Years
  2. Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM): 15 Years
  3. Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD): 14 Years
  4. iShares ESG MSCI KLD 400 ETF (DSI): 12 Years
  5. Invesco Russell Top 200 Equal Weight ETF (EQWL): 12 Years
  6. Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG): 12 Years
  7. iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO): 11 Years
  8. VictoryShares US Large Cap High Div Volatility Wtd ETF (CDL): 11 Years
  9. SPDR S&P 1500 Composite Stock Market ETF (SPTM): 10 Years

Interestingly, what these nine ETFs have in common is low turnover. CDL's and SCHD's last-year turnover rates are the highest at 44% and 30%, respectively, but all others had turnover less than 25% last year, including VIG (11%) and SPY (3%). To me, this is not a coincidence. When you buy a reasonably fixed basket of stocks that don't change much during Index reconstitutions, you reap the rewards when these holdings increase their dividend payments. As a result, it follows that as long as portfolio quality is high and the underlying companies can afford to increase their dividends, they will. There's very little action behind the scenes that would put that logic at risk.

When looking at some fundamentals for these ETFs, a few stand out more than others. I think SPY is a great benchmark, and in terms of combining a high starting dividend yield with strong dividend growth, SCHD is in a league of its own, though its 50.82% five-year total return is lackluster and illustrative of some deeper fundamental issues (more on that later).

VYM is another possible choice, given its 0.04% expense ratio and solid 2.52% estimated dividend yield. However, its 2.03% three-year dividend CAGR is something that will need to turn around if it's going to attract assets from the DGI crowd. There's also DGRO, which seems to offer a nice mix of dividend yield, dividend growth, and capital appreciation. Lastly - and this is a bit of a wildcard - there's EQWL. Its estimated dividend yield and trailing twelve-month payout ratios are almost identical to VIG's, but it has the edge on the three-year dividend CAGR statistic (6.79% vs. 4.49%), 5Y total returns (68.12% vs. 60.37%), and 10Y total returns (257.52% vs. 220.05%).

VIG: Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

Using my ETF Sector Comparison Tool, I generated the following table that shows that VIG overweights Financials and Technology by 5.66% and 4.50% against the peer average consisting of SPY, SCHD, VYM, DGRO, and EQWL. While Financials is a popular sector for a large-cap value ETF, overweighting Technology is a little unusual. That's because VIG's Index is market-cap-weighted with a 4% security cap, which leads to low-yielding mega-cap tech stocks like Broadcom (AVGO), Apple (AAPL), and Microsoft (MSFT) occupying the top three spots on the holdings list.

VIG also has 0% allocated to Communication Services, and if you're familiar with the dividend stock universe, this means no Verizon Communications (VZ) or Comcast (CMCSA), which have dividend growth streaks of 21 and 17 years, respectively. On the surface, their exclusions make no sense, but keep in mind they have indicated yields of 5.73% and 4.73%, which puts them in the top 25% of the Index's eligible stock universe. They're great examples of how VIG's Index goes out of its way to avoid ultra high-yield stocks, presumably because they may be yield traps, while an ETF like SCHD seeks them out.

Lastly, VIG underweights Energy by 4.16% on average, and this has obviously been a headwind in 2026 due to the Iran War. WTI oil prices moved above $100 per barrel for the first time since 2022, but I should point out that VIG still fared pretty well in that negative year for the stock market. As shown below, it escaped with a 9.81% loss that year, which was about half the decline of SPY. Others, particularly VYM, held up much better, and based on my review from December 2021, it headed into that year with about 3x as much Energy sector exposure as SPY.

Next is a list of VIG's top ten holdings, which comprise 32.60% of the portfolio. In addition to the three tech stocks mentioned earlier, VIG holds JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Exxon Mobil (XOM), and due to its weighting scheme, these are all household mega-cap names with dividend yields ranging from 0.51% to 2.56%. On a weighted average basis, VIG's indicated dividend yield is 1.75%, and subtracting the ETF's 0.04% expense ratio is how I arrived at a 1.71% estimated yield figure.

Please note that this holdings list does not match the official list currently on the Vanguard website. However, the website is only updated as of February 28, 2026, and as such, does not reflect the results of the Index reconstitution. To get that data, I accessed the portfolio composition file, which is updated daily, to generate the above table and calculate an accurate set of fundamental statistics. It's a technique I've used for several straight years with success, and in my experience, Vanguard will update its website with its March 31, 2026, allocations around April 15, 2026.

VIG Dividend Characteristics Based on Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades

As you may know, Seeking Alpha provides Quant Dividend Grades for nearly all U.S. stocks, providing insights into a company's dividend safety, growth, yield, and consistency characteristics. Per Seeking Alpha, they "can provide an instant characterization of each stock's dividend strength or weakness compared to its sector," but to date, they are not available for ETFs. One workaround I've done is to normalize these grades on a 1-10 scale in 0.75-point increments (F to A+) and then weight them accordingly for each ETF. This exercise reveals just how strong VIG is from a Dividend Safety, Growth, and Consistency perspective. Its 3.77/10 Dividend Yield Score is a weakness, but that's not VIG's main objective. If you'd like more immediate income, then SCHD, VYM, DGRO, or EQWL are better options.

ETF Dividend Safety Dividend Growth Dividend Yield Dividend Consistency
VIG 8.62 8.37 3.77 8.72
SPY 8.77 8.49 3.22 6.74
SCHD 7.44 7.43 6.65 8.86
VYM 7.81 7.23 5.05 8.10
DGRO 8.35 7.81 4.49 8.48
EQWL 8.13 7.70 4.58 7.68

To put VIG's 8.37/10 Dividend Growth Score into context, it ranks #5/148 among all the large-cap value ETFs I track, so it's excellent evidence that VIG is delivering on its main dividend growth objective. VIG also ranks #6/148 on Dividend Safety and #12/148 on Dividend Consistency but only #139/148 on Dividend Yield. Again, that's the tradeoff, but I'm hoping these scores are insightful and you can use them to find the right mix of ETFs that suit your needs. If you're interested in knowing the scores for other ETFs, please let me know in the comments section at the end.

VIG Fundamental Analysis: Top 20 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for VIG's top 20 holdings, which comprise 48.21% of the fund. Here, we can begin to see why VIG has such strong Dividend Growth and Safety Grades. On a weighted average basis, its holdings have increased dividend payments by an annualized 9.00% over the last three years, which is the highest in the peer group. In addition, its aggregate 37.36% dividend payout ratio means its holdings are not being stretched at all when it comes to making their dividend payments. If anything, there's plenty of room for future growth, especially for mega-cap tech stocks like Apple, whose payout ratio is only 13.02%.

Dividends are paid from earnings, so it follows that to have consistent dividend growth, a company must also have consistent earnings growth. That's where VIG stands out, as its 9.88% 3Y EPS CAGR is second only to SPY, as is its 13.67% next-year EPS growth estimate. In situations where dividend growth exceeds earnings growth, that means the payout ratio will increase, and that's where high-dividend ETFs like SCHD run into trouble. As the table reveals, its components have an aggregate 3Y Dividend CAGR of 7.06% but only a -2.62% 3Y EPS CAGR. It's been this way for several years now, and I'm convinced it's the main reason why the ETF has struggled post-pandemic. People don't want to own companies that aren't growing, but fortunately for VIG investors, that's not an issue.

In terms of quality, all six ETFs listed look pretty solid. However, VIG's 27.39% EBIT margins and 16.63% return on capital rank #2 behind SPY, so I'd say it has the edge over the four dividend ETFs and EQWL. In strong bull markets, that should help bolster returns, and at the same time, VIG's 0.86 five-year portfolio indicates less market risk, which aligns with how it's performed against SPY over the long run. As shown below, VIG's 13.42% annualized standard deviation over the last ten years is the lowest in the peer group, and its -10.34% "Worst 12 Months" result is about 8% better than SPY.

Why Choose VIG Over DGRO?

You may have noticed that DGRO scores well on many of the same metrics as VIG, making it a possible alternative for those seeking a higher starting yield. After all, its five-year beta is lower than VIG's (0.79 vs. 0.86), it trades at a cheaper forward P/E (17.93x vs. 20.29x), it has less allocated to its top 20 holdings (41.94% vs. 48.21%), and its risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios), based on the table above, are virtually identical.

It's a good question, and although I don't have an answer that will satisfy all readers, I want to circle back to VIG's primary investment objective: dividend growth. On that note, my logic is simple:

  • High quality companies are better able to weather economic downturns, and VIG's quality statistics are superior to DGRO's.
  • Sustainable dividend growth should be funded by strong earnings growth, and VIG's EPS growth rates are 2-3 percentage points better than DGRO's.
  • VIG's Dividend Growth Score, as calculated by Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades, ranks #5/148 in its category compared to #23/148 for DGRO.

Therefore, if your primary goal is sustainable long-term dividend growth, then I think VIG is the better choice. However, if a higher starting yield is important to you and you're looking for something a little cheaper and less concentrated, then DGRO is also a good choice.

Investment Recommendation

VIG certainly meets the definition of a high-quality dividend growth ETF. It's one of nine U.S. Size & Style ETFs with a 10+ year dividend growth streak, and based on Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades and the fundamental statistics of its underlying holdings, it's one of the best DGI ETFs on the market. VIG also has great dividend safety and consistency scores that are supported by a 37% portfolio-level dividend payout ratio and a 10+ year dividend growth requirement for its 331 holdings.

The statistics presented today prove that VIG comes as advertised. The only question is whether the strategy itself is appropriate for your needs. I believe it is if dividend growth is one of your primary investment objectives, but if you require something different, such as more growth potential or a higher starting yield, then ETFs like SPY, SCHD, and even DGRO should be considered. Either way, I think a solid "hold" rating is most appropriate for VIG today, and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Viridian Therapeutics: Why Elegrobart Phase 3 Results Fail To Challenge Amgen
data.description:
Viridian Overview

My last analysis of Viridian Therapeutics (VRDN) was way back in September 2023. It came after Phase 1/2 data on its lead asset, VRDN-001, in thyroid eye disease, or TED.

I, alongside the market (the stock dropped 20% immediately following the data), was unimpressed. While VRDN-001 led to some reduction in eye protrusion, it didn’t improve diplopia, or double vision, which is a key feature of TED.

Management planned to advance VRDN-001 into a late-stage program. They were also beginning to evaluate a SC anti-IGF-1R and anti-TSHR antibody, VRDN-003.

I rated VRDN a “Sell,” hoping for “more compelling clinical efficacy and a clear path to market” before a potential re-rating.

Following Phase 3 trial results for elegrobart (formerly VRDN-003) last week, Viridian’s stock has dropped some 50% since. I take another look below.

Recent Developments

Viridian has actually been hit twice with bad news in the past week.

Although the Phase 3 (REVEAL-1) study was technically positive in that it hit statistical significance in the primary endpoint, the market was “underwhelmed by the overall efficacy profile.”

And because Clinical Activity Score failed (p=0.24) in the Q4W arm, every secondary endpoint that followed (i.e., diplopia resolution) was technically only "nominally significant."

The disappointment snowballed on Monday after Amgen (AMGN) unveiled Phase 3 results for its SC version of Tepezza, a direct competitor of Viridian’s.

For context, Tepezza IV is the first and only FDA-approved medication specifically for TED. It blocks IGF-1R and is highly effective, with an 83% proptosis response rate in OPTIC Study 2.

Tepezza SC appears to have similar effectiveness to its IV cousin.

Mind you, Tepezza IV owns this market, generating $1.9B in FY25 revenue. So, Amgen, a big pharma company, shouldn’t have a problem transitioning existing (and attracting new) TED patients to SC.

Of course, Viridian was hoping to improve upon Tepezza with better convenience and potentially superior efficacy, but the latter does not appear to be the case. And I don’t think that most patients will be willing to sacrifice efficacy for 2-5 fewer shots per year.

There’s also a problem with elegrobart’s dose response. The more infrequent dose (Q8W) performed better in proptosis, but worse in diplopia. Moreover, while the more frequent dose (Q4W) was associated with an encouraging 71% diplopia responder rate, it was associated with three times the rate of tinnitus (11.3%) compared to the Q8W arm (2.3%).

While this could merely be "statistical noise” from a small sample size (44 patients per arm), it could also be the result of an extremely long half-life. That is, it is possible that even the Q8W doses “max out” the IGF-1R receptor blockade. And if the receptors are already completely saturated, adding more drug (Q4W) won’t improve the physical recession of the eye and may increase the risk of off-target side effects like tinnitus.

Now, tinnitus is also a "bug" of Tepezza. In fact, current guidelines for Tepezza call for baseline audiograms, ongoing monitoring, and immediate symptom reporting. While this hasn’t limited Tepezza’s TAM, it would've been nice for Viridian to differentiate itself in this regard.

Pipeline

Viridian’s lead IV candidate, veligrotug (formerly VRDN-001) is currently under FDA review (PDUFA June 30, 2026).

However, in THRIVE, veligrotug saw a 70% proptosis response, materially lower than Tepezza in OPTIC. Viridian believes veligrotug’s shorter treatment course and “rapid response” will set it apart from Tepezza. But following Tepezza SC data, this may be a moot point.

When it comes to biologics, transitioning patients from IV to SC can happen quickly. Daratumumab SC, for instance, captured 82.9% of Medicare daratumumab spending within two years of approval. In fact, there is little incentive for TED patients to remain on IV therapy. It’s quite the opposite. IV therapy involves specialized infusion centers and facility fees. And who will want to drive an hour, get an IV, and wait, when they can, instead, administer it from home?

Within this context, both of Viridian’s lead products appear troubled to me. And I haven’t even brought up the fact that Amgen is roughly 100x bigger (with an established presence in TED) than Viridian (market cap).

REVEAL-2 (chronic TED; results expected in Q2) may offer some solace should elegrobart impress there, but I wouldn’t bet on it.

And outside of veligrotug and elegrobart, Viridian’s pipeline looks thin, featuring a preclinical “anti-TSHR” for TED and a Phase 1 “anti-FcRN” (a crowded MOA) in “autoimmune diseases” (a crowded space).

Pharma DCF

I’m going to model veligrotug and elegrobart as one (“TED Franchise”).

My assumptions include 12.5% WACC, 85% POS (NDA stage), 2027 Launch (veligrotug; followed by elegrobart in 2028), 2034 Peak, 2040 LOE, 20k US TED Target Pop. (moderate-to-severe disease; new-start; Viridian estimate), 4% annual Pop. Growth (assumes Viridian will expand Target Pop. via chronic disease uptake), 10% Peak Share (second-to-market), $285k Net Price (blended; a modest discount to Tepezza pricing), $200M in Annual Pre-Launch R&D (just for 2026), 12% COGS (biologic), 70% Launch OpEx for 2 Yrs (heavy S&M investments early on; first drug launch), and a 40% Steady-State OpEx.

I have Viridian’s TED Franchise peaking ~$800M in annual sales and worth $826.6M in risk-adjusted Net Present Value (rNPV).

SOTP

Assuming Virdian’s pipeline outside of these two assets are worth zero in rNPV (which is generally a positive assumption because the vast majority of early-stage drug candidates fail), VRDN appears slightly overvalued.

The Intrinsic Equity Value of $1.65B comes close to Viridian’s Current Market Cap of $1.66B.

$1.65B was derived from adding the TED Franchise (plus a 0.35 ROW Multiple) rNPV to its cash ($800M is a pro forma author estimate that anticipates Q1 burn), minus debt, and divided by its 116.1M Shares Out.

My Fair Value/Share of $14.18 is calculated by dividing the Intrinsic Equity Value by the Diluted Share Count. Because I estimated that Viridian is “Fully Funded,” the Diluted Share Count is simply Shares Out.

Financial Health

Viridian ended FY25 with $875M in cash and investments.

The company “burned” (cash from operations) $276.4M in FY25, giving them a “cash runway” into 2029.

However, as Viridian approaches the launch of veligrotug and elegrobart, investors should expect its OpEx to rise and its cash burn to accelerate.

Conclusion

Viridian appears a bit overvalued based on, what I believe to be, optimistic assumptions (i.e., 10% peak share; meaningfully expanding into chronic TED). Viridian’s TED franchise is not just entering a market with an equal. Amgen is a giant with a stronghold in TED. And I’m afraid Viridian’s incremental product differentiation (i.e., convenience) is not enough to justify the apparent drop in efficacy (cross-trial comparison limitations aside). Moreover, a lot of Viridian’s value is tied up in cash, which will inevitably fade. With both lead assets facing significant headwinds and a thin pipeline, the risk/reward still appears skewed to the downside (maintain “Sell”).

Risks to my thesis include a surprisingly strong REVEAL-2 readout in chronic TED, evidence of strong market adoption of Viridian's TED Franchise, or a major commercial misstep by Amgen.

data.title: Intel's $25B Terafab Deal - Your Chance To Cash Out
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intel Corporation (INTC) recently announced it is joining Elon Musk’s $25B grand vision of making leading-edge chips on American soil.

This announcement has strong long-term impacts for Intel to get its foot back in the game of competing as a leading-edge foundry it once was. At the same time, the Terafab deal gives Intel a potential revenue anchor by bringing in external demand for its 18A process node.

But the earliest production roadmaps for kickstarting chip manufacturing at Terafab are only in 2027, and those roadmaps aren’t even official yet. Meanwhile, Intel is still stuck with an outlook that indicates low single-digit growth.

While I am happy about my massive call on Intel last year, I am downgrading my view on Intel to a neutral stance, advising investors to start fading any rally from here on.

What Tesla’s Terafab Venture Does for Intel

With the arrival of LBT (Lip-Bu Tan, Intel’s current CEO) last year, I was extremely encouraged by LBT’s strategic initiatives to first arrest the rapid margin declines and shore up its balance sheet. At the time, I believed markets were still mispricing Intel’s new roadmap to conserve costs and protect shareholders, which I noted in July last year. Since my upgraded coverages last year, Intel’s shares are up 160%.

There was speculation last year about management cancelling its 18A node due to low yields, something I had previously expressed mixed feelings about. But over the last many months, Intel has made meaningful improvements to the yield on its 18A process node and gotten their production yield to a point where the new Core Ultra Series 3 chips, internally known as "Panther Lake," have already started shipping and will hit mass production in H2 2026. This coincides with the 7-8% per month yield improvement LBT revealed in the Q4 earnings call a few months ago.

Progress on the 18A node was improving management’s confidence about reliability to such an extent that management believed 18A was ready for external customers, as revealed by management at an investor conference last month.

In that conference, management reiterated a recent strategic foundry roadmap—keep Intel’s more advanced 14A node for internal production and the more reliable 18A for external customers while leaning in deeper on ancillary sources of revenue such as their EMIB (Embedded Multi-die Interconnect Bridge) chip packaging. A separate report by Digitimes also revealed that Nvidia (NVDA) would utilize its 5% stake in Intel as leverage to secure Intel’s 18A/14. A manufacturing capacity for its Feynman GPU to be launched in 2028 as well as use Intel’s EMIB.

Finally, we have a pivotal announcement made by Intel yesterday where the company has been signed up as a foundry partner to Tesla’s (TSLA) Terafab vision to help “design, fabricate, and package" 1 terawatt of chips annually. This is great for Intel’s long-term plans to take back control of the flailing narrative that Intel lost its lead as a dominant global chip foundry.

The opportunity to generate revenue by lending its IP to this grand vision of producing chips at the scale of 1 terawatt per year and participating at all crucial stages of the chip-making process is incredible for Intel’s future.

Too Much of the Future Baked Into Intel’s Stock Today

But I believe a majority of the future revenue that markets are excited about is already baked into the stock today.

Take Terafab, for instance. This report by Electrek notes that volume production at Terafab is only slated for CY27, which does not necessarily indicate that revenues from Terafab will be seen in Intel’s topline in CY26. Also, Electrek notes that there is a lot of potential for delay and orchestration, which could impact Terafab’s production timelines, affecting Intel’s ability to recognize revenues from its partnership with the Terafab vision. Electrek cited Tesla’s Battery Day setbacks as an example of how Terafab could see delays.

Moreover, it's extremely challenging to design and manufacture a semiconductor chip, let alone leading-edge chips, at the same time. Few companies have gotten it right in the past, and Intel should know better. Without taking away the credit to Tesla's Elon Musk for addressing the supply problem, the odds of getting Terafab up and running within a year are gargantuan, in my opinion. As this analyst correctly states, there are critical stages such as process integration, lithography, etching, deposition, contamination control, equipment engineering, and the all-important yield management to get this right, and Musk is attempting to do this over the next year at Terafab. Therefore, I believe Intel's stock is running way ahead of itself on just this news.

Intel’s EMIB packaging is also a source of optimism since management revealed in January's Q4 call that their customer engagements on advanced packaging could fetch Intel “well north of $1 billion.” Management’s projections on EMIB revenue echoed this analyst’s previous projection that Intel’s EMIB revenues could touch ~$1 billion in revenues, but that is only expected next year.

At the time of writing, all these announcements, strategic partnerships, and customer engagements have done little to move the needle on the revenue outlook this year, which remains mostly flat. Markets are projecting ~$53-54B in CY26 revenues for Intel.

For now, I believe that Intel has achieved part 1 of its goals to arrest margins and restore confidence to investors of protecting shareholder value.

Given this crucial step that Intel has taken already, I believe markets will switch valuation strategies to now value the company as if its business were as usual, with their sights on Intel’s forward revenues. This is the next part of Intel's goals ahead, in my view.

So far, those revenue estimates have not moved at all, leaving Intel with extremely bloated sales multiples (EV/forward sales) of ~5x FY26 and FY27 sales. Currently, markets are estimating Intel’s CY26 revenues of ~$54B, up 1.6%, while estimating ~$57B in revenues, up 7.5%.

For example, Intel’s global rival, Taiwan Semiconductor, or TSMC (TSM), trades at ~11x CY26 sales expected to grow ~30%. If I directly compare Intel’s forward outlook to TSMC's, I believe Intel should be valued at closer to ~4x forward sales, implying a potential downside of at least 14-15% ahead. My estimation is purely based on the notion that while Intel’s current transformation is clearing the path to a bright future, too much of the future is being priced into today’s value of Intel.

Just like I noticed the mispricing in Intel last year, leading me to upgrade the shares, I am noticing a similar overpricing of the future in Intel, allowing investors to trim some of their exposure to Intel.

Takeaway

While Intel is leading the charge and moving ahead on its goals to deliver a sustained inflection, the resulting exuberance from the investor community is leading to Intel’s shares trading at rich valuation premiums. Intel’s Terafab announcement with Tesla has further exacerbated the richness in those valuation premiums, giving prudent investors a chance to start trimming their exposure as Intel’s shares keep rallying higher.

I am turning neutral with my outlook on INTC shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sabine Royalty Trust Appears Slightly Overvalued
data.description:
About five and a half years ago, I recommended buying Sabine Royalty Trust (SBR) for its exceptional business performance and my expectations for a strong recovery from the pandemic. The stock has vastly outperformed the S&P 500 since that article, as it has offered a total return of 329%, whereas the S&P 500 has doubled. I reiterated my bullish thesis about six months later for similar reasons. Since then, the stock has offered a total return of 285%, whereas the S&P 500 has gained 64%.

Due to the steep rally of Sabine Royalty Trust after these two articles, I lowered my rating from “buy” to “hold” in the fall of 2021. However, the change of the rating proved premature, as the oil and gas trust has offered a total return of 190% since that article (vs. +47% of the S&P 500). About three and a half years ago, I recommended selling Sabine Royalty Trust, primarily due to my expectations that the price of oil would incur a sharp decrease off the highs it recorded shortly after the invasion of Russia in Ukraine. Indeed, the stock has pronouncedly underperformed the broad market since that article, as it has offered a total return of only 31%, whereas the S&P 500 has advanced 75%.

Since then, I have reiterated my sell rating five times with similar success, with the exception of the last two times. Since I reiterated my sell rating in mid-2024 and in September 2024, Sabine Royalty Trust has outperformed the S&P 500 by 11% and 24%, respectively. The outperformance has resulted not only from the unforeseeable war in Iran but also from the strong business performance of the oil and gas trust (more on this later). The big question is whether Sabine Royalty Trust is attractive or investors should look elsewhere for great returns.

Business overview

Sabine Royalty Trust is a high-quality oil and gas trust, which was founded in 1983 and has royalty interests in properties in Florida, Louisiana, Mississippi, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and Texas. At inception, the trust had an expected lifetime of 9-10 years, but it has surpassed that estimate by an impressive margin, as it remains at full strength 43 years after its foundation. This performance record is certainly impressive, particularly given the static status of the assets of the trust, i.e., the trust does not have the right to acquire new properties. The key factor behind the longevity of Sabine Royalty Trust is the great technological progress that has taken place in the recovery of oil.

Due to the natural decline of oilfields, the reserves of an oil and gas trust are expected to decline over the long run. However, Sabine Royalty Trust has exhibited remarkable resilience in this aspect. More precisely, the proven reserves of the trust have grown 40% over the last three years, from 13.856 to 19.433 million barrels of oil equivalent. This is undoubtedly an impressive achievement. The amount of reserves is greatly affected by the prevailing prices of oil and gas, as high prices render some reserves economical to extract, whereas low prices render the same reserves uneconomical. However, the above performance has not resulted from a change in prices, as the price of oil was approximately 20% lower at the end of 2025 than it was at the end of 2022.

In late February, Sabine Royalty Trust reported its results for the full year 2025. Its oil and gas volumes decreased 4% and 17%, respectively, over the prior year. The average realized price of natural gas increased 39%, but the average realized price of oil declined 16%. As a result, royalty income decreased 7%, and total annual distributions dipped 5.5%, from $5.45 to $5.15.

The lackluster performance in 2025 partly resulted from weak oil prices, which were caused by a global supply glut, as OPEC ramped up its production aggressively after years of tight production quotas. However, the situation has completely changed this year. The ongoing war in Iran has caused the price of oil to skyrocket due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Under normal conditions, nearly 20% of global oil output used to pass through these straits. Consequently, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran has caused an unprecedented supply shock.

The sky-high oil prices have not been reflected in the monthly distributions of Sabine Royalty Trust yet, as it takes 2-3 months for the prevailing prices to be reflected in the distributions of the trust. Therefore, Sabine Royalty Trust is likely to greatly increase its distributions in the upcoming months. Based on its latest monthly distribution of $0.3250, the stock is offering an annualized distribution yield of 5.1%, but its yield is likely to increase in the near future thanks to the tailwind of a nearly 3-year high price of oil.

On the other hand, the odds favor a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz sooner or later, as all the parties involved in the ongoing war are bleeding severely, and hence the current situation is unsustainable. Iran is being bombarded harshly while the U.S. government is being hurt by sky-high gasoline prices, which have rendered consumers upset. Even worse, if the price of oil remains excessive for months, it is likely to cause a resurgence of inflation around the globe, including the U.S. Given the upcoming midterm elections, the U.S. government will do its best to prevent a resurgence of inflation. Indeed, since the onset of the war in late February, the U.S. government has shifted its tone and has been trying to put an end to the war.

Moreover, all the oil producers in the Middle East have been forced to drastically reduce their output, as they cannot export it with the straits closed. These countries rely heavily on oil revenues to fund their budgets, and hence they have been suffering from the closure of the straits. To cut a long story short, all the parties involved in the war are bleeding harshly, and hence they are likely to do their best to resolve the crisis. President Trump just announced that he will suspend the attack on Iran for two weeks. While nothing is final yet, this is certainly a major step towards the reopening of Hormuz. The price of oil plunged 16% on the news.

Overall, it is hard to predict when the war in Iran will end, but it is likely that all the countries involved will do their best to terminate the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. When that happens, the price of oil is likely to begin to revert towards its pre-war levels. Therefore, the tailwind from the war in Iran is likely to prove short-lived for Sabine Royalty Trust.

Valuation

As an oil and gas trust, Sabine Royalty Trust distributes its entire net income to its unitholders, and hence it has a payout ratio of 100% (D=E). Therefore, there is no need to examine price-to-earnings ratios (P/E). Instead, the evaluation of the stock can be simply based on the distribution yield (D/P) of the stock. More precisely, one can evaluate the stock by comparing its current distribution yield to its historical average yield.

As mentioned above, Sabine Royalty Trust is currently offering an annualized distribution yield of 5.1%. This yield is lower than the 10-year average distribution yield of 8.2% of the stock.

This means that the stock price will have to decrease for the distribution yield to become aligned with the historical yield. Therefore, the stock appears somewhat richly valued right now.

As mentioned above, the yield of Sabine Royalty Trust is likely to increase significantly in the upcoming months, as the high price of oil will begin to be reflected in the distributions of the trust. However, this rally is unlikely to last beyond this year, given the efforts of the U.S., Iran, and the other countries in the Middle East to put an end to the war.

On the other hand, as mentioned above, Sabine Royalty Trust is a high-quality trust, with an exceptional performance in the growth of reserves. Buying this stock at a price that corresponds to a 5.1% yield is not an extremely risky investing decision. Nevertheless, the stock appears somewhat richly valued, as it is offering a much lower yield than its historical average yield. Given also the high cyclicality of this trust, which results from the cycles of the price of oil, I recommend waiting for a correction of the stock towards the technical support of $65 before considering a purchase.

Final thoughts

The oil industry is characterized by dramatic boom-and-bust cycles. For instance, the price of oil plunged to deep negative levels in 2020 due to the pandemic but surged to a 14-year high in early 2022 due to the onset of the war in Ukraine. Given these dramatic swings, I prefer investing in oil stocks only during downturns. In this way, the investment risk is greatly reduced while the expected return is excessive whenever the recovery of the sector shows up. The 329% total return of Sabine Royalty Trust since my first article, in 2020, reflects the merits of this strategy. While Sabine Royalty Trust does not appear grossly overvalued right now, I recommend waiting for a significantly lower entry point.

data.title: Philip Morris: The Market Is Underpricing The Smoke-Free Transformation
data.description:
Philip Morris International (PM) is executing what I believe is the most important business model transformation in the consumer packaged goods sector today. When I look across the consumer staples segment I see a sector largely characterized by low single-digit organic growth, margin pressure from input costs, and stagnant volume trends.PM is challenging the narrative as its simultaneously growing its top-line at a high single digit organic growth rate while expanding margins through the shift toward smoke-free products which is leading to forward earnings growth in the double digits. That combination is exceptionally rare in this market environment. I believe the bull case for PM is still intact as they have built a multi-year growth narrative around IQOS heated tobacco and ZYN nicotine pouches. As smoke-free volumes grow and margins expand I believe that we will see PM's cash flow increase which could lead to future dividend increases and a repurchase plan being implemented. This isn't a turnaround play and the tobacco narrative should be self correcting as PM has delivered 5 consecutive years of YoY revenue growth since 2020 which is proving their strategic plan and their execution is working. I am bullish on PM and believe that the pullback is an opportunity to lock in a dividend yield that is now over 3.5%.

My previous article about Philip Morris

At the end of September I had written an article on PM (can be read here). I had discussed how PM delivered a strong Q2 with record revenues, robust smoke-free product growth, and expanding operating margins. I felt that despite their operational momentum PM's valuation appeared stretched versus peers Altria Group (MO) and British American Tobacco (BTI) which in my mind made the risk/reward less attractive. After going through their Q4 results and the 2026 guidance I am upgrading my investment thesis back to bullish as I believe that PM's execution on its product and revenue mix beyond combustible tobacco products is working. PM is a leader in the space and while the regulatory and legal risks are significant I still think PM can continue to grow and deliver for shareholders.

Philip Morris delivered an incredible 2025

PM's numbers across every major financial and operational metric reinforce my thesis that its succeeding on its product transformation.PM delivered $40.6 billion in total revenue in 2025 which is up 7.3% YoY and marks the 5th consecutive year of YoY revenue growth. The revenue growth in 2025 was driven by a $1.5 billion favorable pricing variance predominantly from combustible tobacco and $920 million in favorable volume mix driven by higher smoke-free product volumes. PM's gross profit expanded 11.1% to $27.3 billion which is notable because that growth rate materially exceeds the revenue growth rate which reflects the higher margin smokeless products become a larger portion of the revenue mix. PM's bottom lines also grew a dramatic amount as their 2025 EBITDA came in at $18.73 billion which was an increase of 24.8% ($3.72 billion) YoY. PM's net income jumped by 60.80% ($4.29 billion) YoY to $11.35 billion which led to the 2nd consecutive year of double-digit EPS growth.

On an operational front PM was able to increase its shipment volume across cigarettes and smoke free products by 1.4% YoY to 786.5 billion units. This was the 5th consecutive year of volume growth which I am considering a huge achievement from a company that also sells combustible cigarettes in a structurally declining category. PM's smoke free product shipment volumes totaled 179.1 billion units which was up 12.8% YoY as the volume in smoke free pouches increased 18.5% and e-vapor more than doubled. The smoke-free business accounted for 41.5% of total net revenues in 2025 which was up 38.7% YoY and it represented an increase of almost 43% in their total gross profit. PM's smoke-free products are now available in 106 markets and used by an estimated 43 million adult consumers globally which is an increase of roughly 4.5 million people. PM has invested over $16 billion since 2008 in their smoke free division across R&D and commercialization and I believe that it's paying off in spades.

When I look at the regional performance the numbers are enticing. Europe delivered 6.8% organic revenue growth, with adjusted operating income margins expanding to 46.2%. The IQOS HTU adjusted in-market sales grew an estimated 8.6% in 2025 with an acceleration to 10.3% in Q4. Italy returned to double-digit performance while strong growth was delivered in Germany, Greece, Romania, and Spain. Europe surpassed the 50% threshold for net revenues from smoke-free products which is a critical structural milestone. The South and Southeast Asia became a standout margin story as adjusted OI margins increased 350 basis points organically to 34.1%. Revenue grew 6.4% while IQOS HTU adjusted IMS grew an estimated 20.4% in Q4. In East Asia, Australia and the GTR there was 4.6% of organic revenue growth and 300 basis points of organic OI margin expansion to 47.2% Japan is PM's largest IQOS market and adjusted IMS grew an estimated 7.0% for the full year. The Taiwan IQOS launch in Q4 delivered strong results as it penetrated the market capturing 4% market share.

Why PM will continue to gain market share

I thought there was a major notable development from the earnings release as PM is implementing a new organizational model as they have moved from 4 geographic segments to 3 reportable segments. PM will now report on International Smoke-Free, International Combustibles, and U.S which reflects that the smoke-free business is now operating at scale and the U.S. has become a meaningful standalone revenue contributor through ZYN. It looks as if PM's management team wants to provide investors with clearer visibility into the economics of each business unit. I'm viewing this as a positive signal that management is confident enough in the U.S. business to break it out separately and believe that it will help the market better appreciate the standalone value of the ZYN franchise. If I am correct this could help PM get a more favorable rating as the U.S. market opens back up to their business.

In 2025 PM's smoke-free business delivered $16.9 billion in net revenues as it grew 15% YoY. Smoke-free shipment volumes were up 12.8% for the full year while the gross profit surged 20.3% to $11.7 billion. That growth rate materially exceeds the revenue growth rate which demonstrates how powerful the margins of the smoke-free products are. PM is executing on their flywheel as their generating higher volumes to drive manufacturing efficiencies which expand margins and correlate to increased levels of bottom line earnings. I don't think enough people are paying attention to what is happening outside of combustible tobacco because its still considered a taboo industry nut when you look at the business metrics its still extremely profitable.

The Swedish Match acquisition has proven to be a transformative deal as ZYN has become the growth engine in the U.S. for PM. ZYN nicotine pouches have become a juggernaut in the United States as the numbers speak for themselves. U.S. ZYN shipments reached 794 million cans in 2025 which represented a 37% YoY increase. Overall in the Americas region oral SFP shipments totaled 930 million cans which was a 29% increase YoY. Even with the competitive forces from Altria's (MO) on! brand and smaller entrants PM maintained roughly 2/3 of category value share throughout the year at a premium price position.

What makes ZYN's positioning so powerful is the breadth of its consumer sourcing. Management has discussed that ZYN's new users come from cigarettes, vaping, and traditional smokeless products as they each account for roughly a 1/3rd of new adoption. This matters because it means ZYN's addressable market is not limited to a single nicotine category. The pouch category currently represents a high single-digit percentage of total U.S. industry tobacco volumes and the growth trajectory suggests there is a significant runway ahead for PM in the U.S.

IQOS still remains the cornerstone of PM's international smoke-free strategy as the international IQOS HTU shipments grew 11% to 155.1 billion units in 2025.
